coredata.dc:title: Microwave melting rapid synthesis of CQDs/PpPD heterojunctions with efficient microwave electrodeless lamp photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Y.
Wang, H.
Zhao, Y.
Xin, B.
Jiang, B.
coredata.dc:description: A sheet-like carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamine (CQDs/PpPD) nanophotocatalyst with stable structure and excellent photocatalytic performance was prepared by a microwave melting method using glucose and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials. The structures of CQDs/PpPD were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, scanning electron microscope, and transmission electron microscope. The results showed that the as-prepared nanophotocatalyst was a stable composite structure with sheet-like PpPD as the main body and spherical CQDs of 2.0–5.0 nm evenly distributed on its surface. Combined with the valence band spectrum of X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Diffuse reflectance spectra, and photoluminescence characterization analysis, the structure of CQDs/PpPD was determined to be a type II heterojunction, which confirmed that CQDs/PpPD is an excellent photocatalyst material with stronger light absorption capacity and greater electron–hole pair separation efficiency. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PpPD was investigated in the microwave electrodeless lamp photocatalytic system. The results showed that microwave could greatly improve the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PpPD, which could degrade 91.8% of 50 ppm rhodamine B within 10 min, and its degradation efficiency was more than twice that of P25. After four cycles of degradation experiments, the degradation rate remained stable above 88.0%. The photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/PpPD was discussed through active species detection experiments, combined with the structure and performance characteristics of the materials. Taking into consideration the results obtained from this work, CQDs/PpPD prepared by the microwave melting method is a potential high-efficiency photocatalytic material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs-based nanocomposites
Microwave melting synthesis
Photocatalysis
MEDL

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photodegradation upon irradiation with a xenon lamp

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team in University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by Program for Innovative Research Team in University (IRT-1237).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamine.

(a) X-ray diffraction patterns of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD. (b) Fourier transform infrared spectrometer spectra of PpPD, CQDs/PpPD and CQDs. Abbreviation: CQDs/PpPD, carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamine.

(a) Full Survey XPS spectrum of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD. The core-level spectra of CQDs/PpPD (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) N 1s. Abbreviations: CQDs/PpPD, carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamine; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.
SEM imagines of (a) PpPD and (b) CQDs/PpPD; (c–d) high-resolution TEM images of CQDs/PpPD; (e) Diameter distribution of CQDs (the red line is the Gaussian fitting curve).

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CQDs, PpPD, and CQDs/PpPD; (b) PL spectra of the PpPD, CQDs, and CQDs/PpPD excited by 380 nm. Abbreviation: CQDs/PpPD, carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamineUV–vis, ultraviolet visible; PL, photoluminescence.

The degradation efficiency of RhB in different systems: (a) Xe lamp photocatalytic; (b) MEDL photocatalytic; (c) only MW; (d) the recycling runs of CQDs/PpPD for degrading RhB. Abbreviations: RhB, rhodamine B; MEDL, microwave electrodeless lamp.
Effects of different radical scavenging species on the degradation of RhB over CQDs/PpPD.
Possible mechanism for CQDs/PpPD under microwave-assisted photocatalytic system.
Comparison of photocatalytic degradation results of RhB with different catalysts.

row.entry: A 500 W Xe lamp witha 420 nm filter plate

head.ce:title: Microwave melting rapid synthesis of CQDs/PpPD heterojunctions with efficient microwave electrodeless lamp photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sheet-like carbon quantum dots/poly-p-phenylenediamine (CQDs/PpPD) nanophotocatalyst with stable structure and excellent photocatalytic performance was prepared by a microwave melting method using glucose and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials. The structures of CQDs/PpPD were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, scanning electron microscope, and transmission electron microscope. The results showed that the as-prepared nanophotocatalyst was a stable composite structure with sheet-like PpPD as the main body and spherical CQDs of 2.0–5.0 nm evenly distributed on its surface. Combined with the valence band spectrum of X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Diffuse reflectance spectra, and photoluminescence characterization analysis, the structure of CQDs/PpPD was determined to be a type II heterojunction, which confirmed that CQDs/PpPD is an excellent photocatalyst material with stronger light absorption capacity and greater electron–hole pair separation efficiency. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PpPD was investigated in the microwave electrodeless lamp photocatalytic system. The results showed that microwave could greatly improve the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PpPD, which could degrade 91.8% of 50 ppm rhodamine B within 10 min, and its degradation efficiency was more than twice that of P25. After four cycles of degradation experiments, the degradation rate remained stable above 88.0%. The photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/PpPD was discussed through active species detection experiments, combined with the structure and performance characteristics of the materials. Taking into consideration the results obtained from this work, CQDs/PpPD prepared by the microwave melting method is a potential high-efficiency photocatalytic material.

CQDs/PpPD nanophotocatalyst was rapidly prepared by microwave melting. It showed high degradation efficiency and recyclability in the microwave electrodeless lamp (MEDL) catalytic system, and its catalytic mechanism was further discussed.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data were as used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye wastewater, as a kind of harmful industrial wastewater, continues to attract the attention of researchers. The wastewater containing water-soluble reactive dyes is one of the most difficult to treat [1,2]. Rhodamine B (RhB) is a typical representative of reactive dyes widely used in manufacturing industry. It is a common organic pollutant in triphenylmethane derivatives [3]. When toxic and carcinogenic RhB wastewater is directly discharged into natural sewers, it will cause many environmental problems, such as disrupting aquatic ecosystems, destroying biota, and human beings. At present, the treatment methods for RhB wastewater include adsorption, biological, and chemical methods. However, adsorption method can quickly remove dyes from water, but this physical transfer method does not completely solve the problem of environmental pollution; biological methods are not effective in treating wastewater chromaticity and require multiple treatments; chemical methods can cause secondary pollution to the environment [4]. Therefore, it is essential to explore a fast and efficient degradation process of RhB. For the past few years, photocatalytic technology has been considered as one of the effective ways to treat RhB dye wastewater due to its low operating cost, simple operation, and high destruction rate [5].

As a new generation of photocatalytic materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted special attention. CQDs, also known as carbon dots or carbon nanodots, are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with significant fluorescence [6]. They are composed of discrete, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with superfine sizes less than 10 nm [7,8]. CQDs have the advantages of a small particle size, low cost, rich surface functional groups, good stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and other properties and show great potential in wastewater treatment [9–11]. However, as a kind of photocatalyst, CQDs still have problems such as the expandable light absorption range, the light absorption efficiency to be improved, and the need to reduce the carrier recombination rate [12–14]. Therefore, the preparation and modification of CQDs is still one of the research hotspots in this field.

In recent years, conducting polymers have attracted extensive attention as potential precursors of new optoelectronic materials [15]. A conductive polymer with an extended π-conjugated electron system has a high absorption coefficient in the visible light to near infrared range, which is in good agreement with the solar spectrum [16]. In addition, their delocalized conjugated structure facilitates rapid light-induced charge separation [17]. Poly (p-phenylenediamine) PpPD has demonstrated exceptional advantages as a typical conductive polymer, including a high absorption coefficient in the visible part of the spectrum, strong electrical activity, and good environmental stability [18,19]. When the light is suitable, the electrons in its highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level absorb energy and transition to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level. As a polymer, its structure is a macromolecular structure formed by the polymerization of multiple monomers, and its conjugated structure overlaps in space [20], so that electrons will continue to transition within the polymer segment. At this time, if a structure that can transfer electrons and store electrons is attached to its surface, the electrons can be transferred to the structure through intrachain hopping, which is expected to achieve effective separation of electrons and holes. Fortunately, CQDs happen to be photocatalytic materials with transfer electrons and store them ability [6]. Based on the above prediction, this paper designs to combine PpPD and CQDs to prepare a new composite photocatalyst with high efficiency, which can effectively separate electrons and holes.

P-phenylenediamine (PPDA) monomer has high activity and fast oxidation rate. Therefore, there are many methods to prepare PpPD, such as template method, interfacial polymerization method, initiator guidance method, and so on [21–23]. Literature studies show that the morphology and structure of PpPD obtained by different synthesis methods are often different. Among them, the nanosheet structure can significantly improve the crystallinity of the polymer and increase the dispersion of the polymer in the solution. This is very helpful for photocatalysts that degrade organic pollutants in water. Therefore, in this paper, using glucose and PPDA as raw materials, a novel microwave melting method is attempted to prepare sheet-like PpPD while anchoring CQDs on the polymer surface. Thus, the goal of synthesizing a composite photocatalyst with good light absorption performance and efficient electron–hole separation ability is achieved. Considering that the prepared composite material has good microwave absorption ability [24,25], the photocatalytic degradation effect of this material on organic pollutants will be investigated in the microwave electrodeless lamp (MEDL) catalytic system.

Using the ability of PPDA and glucose to absorb microwaves, they are rapidly melted by microwave heating and provide a high-temperature solvent environment for each other. While realizing the polymerization of PPDA to form sheet-like PpPD, the crystallization of CQDs was completed and anchored on the surface of PpPD. This not only avoids the problem of adding auxiliary reagents such as initiators in traditional polymerization but also obtains CQDs/PpPD with stable physicochemical properties in one step.

The device for preparing the CQDs/PpPD material was a microwave atmospheric heating reflux device. A certain proportion of glucose and PPDA were placed in a three-neck flask, and 1.0 mL of deionized (DI) water was added. The three-neck flask was placed in a microwave synthesizer. The microwave power of the reaction was set to 500 W, the reaction temperature was 150 °C, and the reaction time was 9.0 min. During this period, PPDA and glucose in the raw materials absorbed microwave melting, provided a solvent environment for each other, and reacted with each other to form a high viscosity brown liquid. After the reaction, the brown liquid was dissolved in DI water and cooled to room temperature. Then, we put the mixed solution into the dialysis tubes (molecular weight: 3000 Da) and purified for 72 h. After dialysis, the solution was dried to solid under vacuum at 60 °C. The obtained solid was washed with dilute nitric acid (10 mmol/L) to realize proton doping, thereby enhancing the conductivity of PpPD. This step can effectively improve the charge delocalization ability of the material [26]. Finally, the dry solid was calcinated at 200 °C for 2.0 h. The preparation process of CQDs/PpPD composite is shown in Fig. 1 . All the reagents used in this study were of analytical grade.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using Rigaku Mini-flflex600 diffractometer with CuKα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Frontier, PE) was employed to determine the functional groups on sample surface. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) analysis was carried out on a VG ESCALAB MKII XPS with Mg Kα excitation (1253.6 eV). UV–vis diffuse-reflection spectra were measured on Lambda 950. The morphology and structure of samples were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; Hitachi S-4800) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-2100F). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at room temperature by using Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The models of MEDL photocatalytic reactor (Fig. S1) and electrodeless lamp used in this paper are the same as in the literature [24]. MEDL can generate UV–Vis radiation by absorbing microwave energy [27]. The main radiations are 251, 264, 295, 299, 313, 366, 403, 436, 546, and 577 nm UV–vis light [28,29]. The customized MEDL in this paper is a cylindrical lamp 5.0 cm long and 1.4 cm in diameter, which is made of quartz glass and filled with mercury (5.0 mg) and argon (0.267 Kpa). Under 400 W microwave irradiation, the MEDL emitted 251 nm and 366 nm light intensities of 1.56 and 0.08 mW/cm2, respectively.

The degradation of RhB was chosen as a model reaction for comparing the catalytic performance of different samples. 20 mg of catalyst was dispersed in RhB solution (50 mL, 50 mg/L) and magnetically stirred in the dark for 20 min to attain the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The microwave power was set to 400 W and the reaction time to 10 min. Throughout the degradation process, 3.0 mL of the solution was sampled at 2.0 min intervals and centrifuged to remove the catalyst. The RhB concentration was determined by measuring the absorbance value at the maximum peak using a UV–Vis (Labtech 9100B) scanning spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation upon irradiation with a xenon lamp
ce:section.ce:para: The preliminary preparation is the same as the above experiment. A xenon lamp (300 W) was used as the simulated sunlight source in the photodegradation process. At specific time intervals, 3.0 mL of the solution was taken and the catalyst was centrifuged. The RhB concentration was determined by measuring the absorbance value at the maximum peak using a UV–Vis scanning spectrophotometer.

The recyclability of the catalyst sample was tested under the same experimental conditions as described above. After each degradation reaction, the catalyst sample was centrifuged, washed with DI water and ethanol, dried in oven, and then subjected to the next RhB photocatalytic degradation cycle. The cyclic degradation experiment was repeated four times.

The XRD patterns of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD were shown in Fig. 2 a. By comparing the diffraction peaks of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD, the diffraction peak of pure CQDs appears at 18.3°, corresponding to the graphite carbon (002) plane [13,30]. When CQDs are combined with PpPD, the diffraction peak's strength decreases, the d-spacing becomes wider, and the diffraction peak of the (002) crystal plane shifts 4.8° to a larger angle of 23.1°. This shift may be caused by the substitution of some C elements in the graphite structure with N elements [31]. XRD results show that the composite structure of CQDs/PpPD is formed.

PpPD, CQDs, and CQDs/PpPD samples were detected using FTIR, as shown in Fig. 2b. The PpPD polymer has a large number of characteristic peaks. The intensive peaks between 3376 cm−1, 3304 cm−1, and 3198 cm−1 are attributed to the N–H stretching vibrations of the –NH2 and –NH– groups. The absorption band at 1629 cm−1 is ascribed to the CC stretching vibrations in the benzene ring. The N–H stretching vibrations are responsible for the strong absorption band at 1516 cm−1. The peak at 1263 cm−1 is related to the C–N stretching vibration. In addition, an out-of-plane flexural vibration characteristic absorption peak of the C–H bond is observed at 832 cm−1 [32–35]. In the spectra of CQDs/PpPD, we observe the disappearance of the adsorption peaks at 3376 cm−1, 3304 cm−1, and 3198 cm−1, which is the key evidence that CQDs and PpPD are interconnected. The CC stretching vibration is related with the peak at 1607 cm−1. The peak at approximately 1511 cm−1 proves the existence of amide bonds, corresponding to N–H bonds. This peak is present in the reference PpPD spectrum (very strong). The peak at 1252 cm−1 representing specific C–N bond stretching vibrations in quinone imine units also indicate the formation of CQDs/PpPD. Compared with pure PpPD, the quinoid imine unit in the composite material displays a slight blueshift [32]. This is due to hydrogen bond and π-π bond conjugation during the combination process, which affect the vibrational frequency of the entire molecular chain, resulting in a blue shift. In addition, an out-of-plane flexural vibration characteristic absorption peak of the C–H bond is detected at 824 cm−1, indicating that the CQDs/PpPD has a basic phenazine skeleton. CQDs spectra show the peaks located at 3411 cm−1, 1645 cm−1, and 1027 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH, CC bond (skeleton vibration of aromatic rings), and C–O bonds [36,37]. In CQDs/PpPD, the characteristic absorption peak of the stretching vibration of the O–H bond is observed at 3360 cm−1. Among them, the C–O peak located at 1011 cm−1 in CQDs/PpPD is attenuated relative to the peak in the CQDs spectra. An explanation for this attenuation is that the addition of amino groups to the composite material weakens the signal of oxygen-containing groups. The primary distinctive peaks of the two components can be found in the spectrum of the composite material. From the analysis of the aforementioned results, CQDs and PpPD have been successfully combined to obtain CQDs/PpPD. In addition, the FTIR results show that the surface of the synthesized CQDs/PpPD is rich in hydroxyl groups (3360 cm−1) and amine functional groups (1500 cm−1 and 1252 cm−1), which contributes to the uniform and stable dispersion of CQDs/PpPD in water.

To determine the surface contents and chemical composition of the CQDs/PpPD, XPS was performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XPS spectrum of CQDs/PpPD exhibited a typical C 1s peak at 284.6 eV, an N 1s peak at 400.0 eV and an O 1s peak at 532.5 eV. These results indicate that the main elements of CQDs/PpPD are carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, while only carbon and oxygen elements are detected for CQDs. The corresponding contents of the elements C, N, and O are calculated to be approximately 66.55%, 10.11%, and 23.34%, respectively. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 3b, the C 1s XPS spectrum has three peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.6 eV, and 287.8 eV, which are attributed to the C–C band, C–N band, and C–O band, respectively [35]. Fig. 3c further shows the spectrum of O 1s. The result reveals the C–O band for the carbon hydroxyl groups at 532.7 eV and the O–H band at 531.2 eV [38]. According to the high-resolution N 1s spectra of CQDs/PpPD (Fig. 3d), two main types of nitrogen were doped. The peak at 400.2 eV is attributed to the C–N bond in phenazine, and the peak at 398.8 eV is ascribed to the –NH− bond in benzimidazole groups [33]. The appearance of N and O in CQDs/PpPD clearly indicates that both PPDA and glucose are involved in the formation of the composite material. Based on these results, CQDs/PpPD have been successfully prepared.

The morphology of CQDs/PpPD was studied by SEM and TEM. Pure PpPD exhibits an irregular block structure with a size ranging from 3.0 to 10 μm (Fig. 4 a). After forming a complex with CQDs, CQDs/PpPD is still a composite based on PpPD, in which the morphology of PpPD becomes irregular flakes and its size is reduced to 0.5–1.0 μm (Fig. 4b). The prepared samples were characterized using TEM to further understand the state of CQDs on the polymer surface and the corresponding images. TEM images of CQDs/PpPD show that spherical CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of the PpPD (Fig. 4c), and substantial aggregation is not observed between single CQDs. The lattice fringe structure of CQDs is clearly visible in the high-resolution TEM image. The lattice spacing is 0.34 nm (Fig. 4d), which corresponds to the graphite crystal plane (002) [39]. We used approximately 30 nanospheres to make a diameter distribution map, and the average diameter of the prepared CQDs is 2.88 nm (Fig. 4e). Therefore, the results of the SEM and TEM demonstrate that the CQDs/PpPD prepared using the microwave melting technique has an irregular sheet structure in which spherical CQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of the PpPD. According to the literature, compared with other shapes of PpPD, flake PpPD has better dispersion in water, which is undoubtedly another advantage of the microwave melting method for the preparation of CQDs/PpPD materials.

The UV–Vis DRS spectra of CQDs, PpPD, and the CQDs/PpPD were investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 a. CQDs main exhibit absorption of UV light. In addition to the two absorption peaks in the UV region, PpPD exhibits an absorption peak of approximately 500 nm of visible light. When CQDs and PpPD are combined, the CQDs/PpPD composite absorbs light in the spectral range of 200–800 nm. Its absorption intensity is stronger than the original absorption intensity of CQDs and PpPD. Therefore, the combination of CQDs and PpPD not only expands the light absorption range of the material but also increases its light absorption intensity. Based on this result, we inferred that the use of CQDs/PpPD as a photocatalyst will play a positive role in improving its photocatalytic performance.

The separation, transfer, and recombination processes of photogenerated carriers in photocatalytic reactions have been demonstrated using photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in the results presented in Fig. 5b, both PpPD and CQDs exhibit a certain level of fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity of PpPD is stronger. However, after the two materials were combined, the fluorescence of CQDs/PpPD decreases to a large extent.

Combined with the results of DRS characterization, the capacity of CQDs/PpPD to absorb UV and visible light is improved compared with CQDs and PpPD, but its fluorescence intensity is substantially reduced. The justification for this result is related to the electron–hole pair separation mechanism caused by the change in the composite structure. The electrons of the HOMO of PpPD in the material are excited to the LUMO by light, leaving holes in the HOMO of PpPD [40,41]. CQDs evenly distributed on the surface of CQDs/PpPD are excellent electron acceptors [42,43]. They effectively transfer the electrons transiting to the PpPD LUMO to CQDs, so as to realize enrichment of electrons on CQDs and the retention of holes on PpPD materials. This process successfully and effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, thus greatly reducing their fluorescence intensity. This is exactly in line with our original intention of designing the CQDs/PpPD material, and the change of this characteristic will undoubtedly effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of the material.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared CQDs/PpPD, experiments were carried out to degrade RhB in Xe lamp, MEDL photocatalytic system, and pure microwave system, respectively, and P25 and CQDs were used as control samples. The results are shown in Fig. 6 a, b and 6c. Comparing the results in the figures, it can be seen that only under the action of microwave, the three photocatalytic materials cannot effectively degrade organic pollutants (Fig. 6c). This is because the microwave energy is not enough to excite the photocatalytic material to produce the photocatalytic effect [44]. Under Xe lamp irradiation, all three materials have certain photocatalytic ability to degrade RhB. The photocatalytic degradation rates of RhB by P25, CQDs, and CQDs/PpPD were 48.0%, 8.0%, and 12.0%, respectively, after irradiation for 100 min. Among them, P25 has the strongest photocatalytic ability, followed by CQDs/PpPD, and CQDs exhibits the lowest performance (Fig. 6a). Here, the photocatalytic effect of CQDs/PpPD is only one fifth that of P25. This indicates that the performance of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD under visible light irradiation is inferior to P25.

However, when the three catalysts were applied in the MEDL system, their photocatalytic performance had unexpected changes. First, P25 achieved the same photocatalytic effect in 10min as that in the Xe lamp system for 100 min. In other words, its photocatalytic efficiency is increased by 10 times. Second, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs and CQDs/PpPD overtook that of P25, especially CQDs/PpPD, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB reaches 91.8% within 10 min, almost twice that of P25 (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, the results of the reaction kinetics of different catalysts for RhB degradation reveal that the k value of CQDs/PpPD is approximately 2.9 and 3.36 times higher than that of CQDs and P25, respectively (Fig. S2).

Combined with the structure and performance characteristics of each photocatalytic material, the reasons for this result are analyzed: (1) MEDL is a composite light source whose emission spectrum includes ultraviolet and visible light. Therefore, under the irradiation of this light source, the performance of the three photocatalysts has been greatly improved. (2) Because both CQDs and CQDs/PpPD absorb microwave and convert microwave energy into heat energy, many hotspots may form on the surface of the two catalysts. These hot regions effectively promote an efficient photocatalytic reaction around them [45,46]. Therefore, for these two catalysts, microwave and the electroless lamp exert a synergistic effect, and thus, the catalytic activity of these two catalysts in the MEDL system exceeds that of P25. (3) Combined with the previous characterization results, CQDs/PpPD has a higher separation efficiency of electron–hole pairs than CQDs. Therefore, CQDs/PpPD shows the highest catalytic performance in the MEDL system, which is far better than that of P25.

A recycling experiment was conducted under identical conditions to examine the stability of the CQDs/PpPD photocatalyst. The results in Fig. 6d show that after four cycles of RhB degradation, the rate of RhB degradation by CQDs/PpPD is always stable at more than 88.0%. This result proves that CQDs/PpPD is a stable photocatalyst, and it can be effectively recovered and reused.

Compared with the photocatalysts in the literature (Table 1 ), it can be found that the photocatalytic conversion of the catalysts in the MEDL system is generally higher than that under xenon lamp, and the CQDs/PpPD obtained in this study has more significant advantages than other catalysts. In the photocatalysis experiments in Table 1, the amount of CQDs/PpPD catalyst prepared in this work is only 1/25–4/5 of that of similar catalysts in the literature, the concentration of RhB that can be degraded is 1.7–5.0 times that of other studies, and the conversion rate of photocatalysis is 2.0–7.0 times that of similar catalysts. It can be seen that CQDs/PpPD in the MEDL photocatalytic degradation system not only reduces the catalyst dosage and shortens the degradation reaction time, but also greatly improves the degradation efficiency. The results show that the CQDs/PpPD synthesized by microwave melting method has obvious advantages and broad prospects in the field of photocatalysis compared with other studies, especially in the use of MEDL irradiation.

According to the photocatalytic degradation experiment, CQDs/PpPD shows excellent photocatalytic performance in the MEDL photocatalytic system. The role of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB were investigated using a radical scavenger experiment. Different scavengers, BQ for •O2 −, EDTA for h
+ , AgNO3 for e −, and t-BuOH for •OH, were added to the system to remove the corresponding radicals [50]. As shown in Fig. 7 , t-BuOH caused a slight decrease in removal efficiency. In contrast, after the addition of BQ, EDTA, and AgNO3, the degradation efficiency of RhB dropped to 32.1%, 3.6%, and 26.0%, respectively. Based on these results, the active species e −, h +, and •O2 − play the key roles in the degradation of RhB by CQDs/PpPD in the MEDL photocatalytic system.

The bandgap structure has been roughly calculated based on the valence band spectrum measured (Fig. S3) and the energy bandgap (Fig. S4) by XPS and DRS characterization results, and a schematic diagram of the energy level structure are shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen from the results in the figure that PpPD and CQDs form a type II heterojunction, electrons are transferred from the LUMO energy level of PpPD to the conduction band of CQDs, and holes are transferred from the valence band of CQDs to the HOMO energy level of PpPD. Therefore, the goal of enriching electrons on CQDs and holes on PpPD is achieved, so as to realize the effective separation of electron–hole pairs, which is confirmed by fluorescence characterization. This charge separation mechanism fully conforms to our original intention of designing this composite material.

Combined with the above analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/PpPD in MEDL system is proposed. The results are shown in Fig. 9 . First, MEDL emits UV and visible light under microwave irradiation. The prepared CQDs/PpPD can effectively absorb this light energy to generate electron transitions and complete the separation of electron–hole pairs according to the abovementioned separation mechanism of type II heterojunction [51–53]. Second, some of the electrons enriched on the surface of CQDs directly interact with the dye molecules in the system and rapidly degrade them; the other part reacts with the oxygen in the system to form superoxide anion radicals, which further attack the dye molecules and decompose them through the free radical mechanism. Meanwhile, the holes enriched on the surface of PpPD react with the organic pollutants adsorbed on PpPD and participate in the degradation process. The h + potential on VB of PpPD (1.10 eV vs NHE) is much more negative than the standard potential of H2O/•OH (2.4 eV vs NHE). This means that h + preferentially oxidizes RhB rather than H2O/OH− to form •OH [54]. Therefore, h + is one of the main active species, while •OH plays a secondary role. Therefore, e −, h +, and •O2 − are the main active species for the catalytic degradation of RhB by CQDs/PpPD in this experiment.

Finally, and most importantly, the reaction of degrading organic pollutants is a thermochemical process that conforms to the Arrhenius equation, and the reaction rate increases with increasing temperature. PpPD and CQDs are excellent microwave absorbing materials, they can absorb microwave energy in MEDL catalytic system to form multiple hotspots on their surfaces, which become high-temperature active sites. This can greatly improve the interfacial reaction temperature, thereby effectively promoting the photocatalytic degradation reaction. That is, it can be concluded that in the MEDL catalytic system, CQDs/PpPD exhibited higher photocatalytic activity under the synergistic effect of light and microwave irradiation.

The flake CQDs/PpPD nanophotocatalyst was prepared by a microwave melting method. In the microwave heating system, glucose, and PPDA can absorb microwave and melt, providing each other with a high-temperature thermal solvent environment. During the reaction, the polymerization of PPDA and the process of glucose generating CQDs and anchoring on the surface of PpPD were simultaneously realized. This preparation method not only simplifies the process but also avoids the problems of equipment corrosion and solvent pollution caused by solvents and oxidants in traditional polymerization reactions.

Through characterization analysis, the catalyst was determined to be type II heterojunction structure, and the UV and fluorescence characterization confirmed that CQDs/PpPD was an excellent photocatalyst material with stronger light absorption capacity and higher electron–hole pair separation efficiency. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PpPD was investigated in the MEDL photocatalytic system. Within 10 min, the degradation rate of CQDs/PpPD to 50 ppm RhB solution reaches 91.8%, which was more than twice that of P25. After four cycles of degradation experiments, the degradation rate remained stable above 88.0%. Combined with the detection results of active species detection and the characterization of structure and performance, the reasons and mechanisms for the efficient degradation of organic pollutants by CQDs/PpPD were hypothesized. It is concluded that CQDs/PpPD with type II heterostructures have high carrier separation efficiency in the MEDL catalytic system, which can efficiently degrade organic pollutants under the synergistic effect of light and microwave.

Ying Yang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review and editing, Formal analysis. Yubo Zhao: Investigation. Hong Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review and editing, Funding acquisition. Baifu Xin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Baojiang Jiang: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by Program for Innovative Research Team in University (IRT-1237).
